text:
The successful synthesis of hybrid porous nanowire arrays made from strongly coupled $\text{Co}_3\text{O}_4$ and carbon by heating the MOF precursors at 800 °C is reported. Such arrays possess high surface area (up to $251\text{ m}^2\text{ g}^{-1}$) and rich carbon content (up to 52.1 wt%), making them suitable as a self-supporting binder-free electrode for the oxygen evolution reaction (OER). As demonstrated in both 0.1 and 1.0 M KOH solutions, the electrode shows a low onset potential of 1.47 V vs. RHE and a remarkable durability with a stable current density of $10.0\text{ mA cm}^{-2}$ at 1.52 V for over 30 hours with a Faradaic efficiency of 99.3%.

Oxide nanowire materials with enhanced performance have been obtained for fuel cell application. The synthesis route described here allows for the production of nanowire arrays of particular crystallographic orientation with intrinsic catalytic properties and resistance to corrosion superior to many existing noble and transition metal catalysts. The exceptional catalytic performance and stability of these oxide nanowire arrays are owing to a synergetic combination of nanostructure and in situ formed carbon. Indeed, such materials enable access to a large surface area, facile transport of reactants and products, an easy detachment of the dissolved oxygen produced at the catalyst surface as well as mechanical strength. The $\text{Co}_3\text{O}_4$–carbon hybrid material is shown to catalyze the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) via a favored four-electron process, thus opening routes for rechargeable battery technology of metal-air batteries and for different clean energy applications such as regenerative fuel cells.
